entry.rationale: The 1880 census is the first federal enumeration to explicitly state each person's relationship to the head of household. If Thomas Flynn was alive and any children remained at home, they would be labeled 'son' or 'daughter' — direct evidence of the parent-child relationship absent from both the 1850 and 1860 censuses. If Patrick (age ~35) had moved out, other siblings still in Thomas's household are identified as FAN targets for step 6. Fills the major 1860–1908 timeline gap flagged in the proof summary.

The 1870 census records the Thomas Flynn household ten years after the confirmed 1860 enumeration. Patrick (age ~25) may still be co-resident, corroborating continued membership in Thomas's household; if he has moved out, other Flynn children still at home are identified as siblings — the primary FAN targets for step 6. The 1870 enumeration page also surfaces neighbors who may belong to the same Irish immigrant community as Thomas Flynn, broadening the associate cluster.

Schuylkill County's Irish Catholic mining community was served by established parishes whose registers are partly indexed in FamilySearch collection 1401638 (Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985; 1.2 million records). A baptism entry for Patrick or any of his siblings naming Thomas Flynn as father would be primary direct evidence of parentage — the strongest evidence class available for this question. Patrick's own marriage entry in a church register would also name his father. Search Flynn in Schuylkill County parishes, ca. 1840-1870.

Browse-only image group 007936751 (187 images, bilingual English/German, 0% indexed, 1842-1895) contains Schuylkill County church records digitized but never transcribed into any index, invisible to name searches. If step 3 returns nothing, browsing these images may recover baptism or marriage register pages naming Thomas Flynn as father. Because Irish Catholic registers are sometimes interleaved with German Lutheran registers in Schuylkill County, both language sections should be examined.

Pennsylvania civil and county marriage records from the late 19th century often name the parents of both parties. A marriage record for Patrick Flynn — who must have married before 1908, when his son-in-law served as informant on his death certificate — would name Thomas Flynn as his father, constituting direct evidence supplied by Patrick or his bride. Search FamilySearch collections 1921317 (Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885-1950) and 1571957 (Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677-1950), both indexed. Also check browse-only volume 007936753 (Schuylkill County marriage records, 94 images, 0% indexed, 1870-1910) if the indexed search fails.

Core FAN strategy: identify Patrick's siblings from the 1870 and 1880 census results (steps 1-2), then search for their death certificates in FamilySearch collection 2255317 (Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968; 4.2 million indexed records). Each sibling's death certificate independently names Thomas Flynn as father, providing corroborating direct evidence from multiple documents. Multiple sibling certificates converging on the same father constitute strong cumulative evidence even if each individual certificate has a secondary informant.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland before the 1850 census. His naturalization petition — filed with the Schuylkill County Court of Common Pleas — may name his county of origin in Ireland, his approximate age, his arrival date, and sometimes family members in the United States. Browse-only volume 007936750 (298 images, 0% indexed, 1849-1915) must be examined image by image. Establishing Thomas's Irish origin strengthens his identity as Patrick's father and may open a path to Irish civil registration or parish baptism records that would constitute primary evidence of parentage.

Land transactions for Thomas Flynn may explicitly name family members. Adult children commonly joined a father in signing a deed of sale to release dower rights, and heirs are named in deeds following a parent's death. A deed naming both Thomas and Patrick Flynn would constitute independent direct evidence of the parent-child relationship. Volume 007936752 (543 images, 22% indexed, 1815-1900) should be searched by index first; if no hits appear, browse the unindexed 78% of images in the relevant time window (ca. 1850-1895).

The earlier indexed probate search (pli_006, log_006) returned nothing — but that search covered only the indexed portion of Schuylkill County probate. Browse-only volume 007936749 (412 images, 0% indexed, 1851-1930) contains images never transcribed into any index. Thomas Flynn's will or letters of administration, if present, would name his children — the strongest possible direct evidence of parentage. Browse the images in the window when Thomas likely died (estimated 1870-1895 based on census appearances) to recover what the previous indexed search missed.

The Miners' Journal (Pottsville, published continuously from 1825) and other Schuylkill County papers routinely published obituaries naming surviving children and sometimes parents. Thomas Flynn's obituary would explicitly name his children, confirming Patrick's parentage. Patrick Flynn's own obituary (d. 12 March 1908) may also name his father or surviving siblings. Ancestry's Historical Newspapers collection includes Pennsylvania titles from this period; the known death date provides a precise anchor for searching March 1908 issues of the Miners' Journal.

The 1900 census fills the final major timeline gap and serves the FAN strategy in two ways: (1) it reports years married and children born and living, allowing Patrick's marriage year and spouse's name to be estimated — directly feeding the marriage-record search in step 5; (2) it identifies Patrick's 1900 neighbors, who may be long-established Schuylkill County Irish families from the same community as Thomas Flynn and therefore traceable in the same church and naturalization records.

entry.rationale: The 1880 census is the first federal enumeration to explicitly state each person's relationship to the head of household. If Thomas Flynn was alive and any children remained at home, they would be labeled 'son' or 'daughter' — direct evidence of the parent-child relationship absent from both the 1850 and 1860 censuses. If Patrick (age ~35) had moved out, other siblings still in Thomas's household are identified as FAN targets for step 6. Fills the major 1860–1908 timeline gap flagged in the proof summary.

The 1870 census records the Thomas Flynn household ten years after the confirmed 1860 enumeration. Patrick (age ~25) may still be co-resident, corroborating continued membership in Thomas's household; if he has moved out, other Flynn children still at home are identified as siblings — the primary FAN targets for step 6. The 1870 enumeration page also surfaces neighbors who may belong to the same Irish immigrant community as Thomas Flynn, broadening the associate cluster.

Schuylkill County's Irish Catholic mining community was served by established parishes whose registers are partly indexed in FamilySearch collection 1401638 (Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985; 1.2 million records). A baptism entry for Patrick or any of his siblings naming Thomas Flynn as father would be primary direct evidence of parentage — the strongest evidence class available for this question. Patrick's own marriage entry in a church register would also name his father. Search Flynn in Schuylkill County parishes, ca. 1840-1870.

Browse-only image group 007936751 (187 images, bilingual English/German, 0% indexed, 1842-1895) contains Schuylkill County church records digitized but never transcribed into any index, invisible to name searches. If step 3 returns nothing, browsing these images may recover baptism or marriage register pages naming Thomas Flynn as father. Because Irish Catholic registers are sometimes interleaved with German Lutheran registers in Schuylkill County, both language sections should be examined.

Pennsylvania civil and county marriage records from the late 19th century often name the parents of both parties. A marriage record for Patrick Flynn — who must have married before 1908, when his son-in-law served as informant on his death certificate — would name Thomas Flynn as his father, constituting direct evidence supplied by Patrick or his bride. Search FamilySearch collections 1921317 (Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885-1950) and 1571957 (Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677-1950), both indexed. Also check browse-only volume 007936753 (Schuylkill County marriage records, 94 images, 0% indexed, 1870-1910) if the indexed search fails.

Core FAN strategy: identify Patrick's siblings from the 1870 and 1880 census results (steps 1-2), then search for their death certificates in FamilySearch collection 2255317 (Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968; 4.2 million indexed records). Each sibling's death certificate independently names Thomas Flynn as father, providing corroborating direct evidence from multiple documents. Multiple sibling certificates converging on the same father constitute strong cumulative evidence even if each individual certificate has a secondary informant.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland before the 1850 census. His naturalization petition — filed with the Schuylkill County Court of Common Pleas — may name his county of origin in Ireland, his approximate age, his arrival date, and sometimes family members in the United States. Browse-only volume 007936750 (298 images, 0% indexed, 1849-1915) must be examined image by image. Establishing Thomas's Irish origin strengthens his identity as Patrick's father and may open a path to Irish civil registration or parish baptism records that would constitute primary evidence of parentage.

Land transactions for Thomas Flynn may explicitly name family members. Adult children commonly joined a father in signing a deed of sale to release dower rights, and heirs are named in deeds following a parent's death. A deed naming both Thomas and Patrick Flynn would constitute independent direct evidence of the parent-child relationship. Volume 007936752 (543 images, 22% indexed, 1815-1900) should be searched by index first; if no hits appear, browse the unindexed 78% of images in the relevant time window (ca. 1850-1895).

The earlier indexed probate search (pli_006, log_006) returned nothing — but that search covered only the indexed portion of Schuylkill County probate. Browse-only volume 007936749 (412 images, 0% indexed, 1851-1930) contains images never transcribed into any index. Thomas Flynn's will or letters of administration, if present, would name his children — the strongest possible direct evidence of parentage. Browse the images in the window when Thomas likely died (estimated 1870-1895 based on census appearances) to recover what the previous indexed search missed.

The Miners' Journal (Pottsville, published continuously from 1825) and other Schuylkill County papers routinely published obituaries naming surviving children and sometimes parents. Thomas Flynn's obituary would explicitly name his children, confirming Patrick's parentage. Patrick Flynn's own obituary (d. 12 March 1908) may also name his father or surviving siblings. Ancestry's Historical Newspapers collection includes Pennsylvania titles from this period; the known death date provides a precise anchor for searching March 1908 issues of the Miners' Journal.

The 1900 census fills the final major timeline gap and serves the FAN strategy in two ways: (1) it reports years married and children born and living, allowing Patrick's marriage year and spouse's name to be estimated — directly feeding the marriage-record search in step 5; (2) it identifies Patrick's 1900 neighbors, who may be long-established Schuylkill County Irish families from the same community as Thomas Flynn and therefore traceable in the same church and naturalization records.

Now I have everything I need. Let me assess the planning mode and what to build.

**Mode check:** Both existing plans for q_001 (pl_002) are `completed`; q_001 is still `in_progress` (only `probable`, not `proved`). This is **add-new mode** — I need a fresh plan targeting the three unsearched censuses.

**Key design decisions:**
- Search Patrick Flynn AND Thomas Flynn independently in each census year — finding Thomas gives a complete household picture even if Patrick has moved out
- The 1880 census is the most valuable: it's the first to record each member's **relationship to the head** — if Patrick and Thomas are co-resident, the relationship would be stated explicitly
- The 1900 census captures **father's birthplace** and **mother's birthplace**, which would corroborate the Ireland origin consistent with the Thomas Flynn hypothesis
- Fallbacks to Ancestry for each year catch variant transcriptions under a different indexing scheme

Now adding the 9 items in sequence.All 9 items validated and written. Here is the complete plan.

---

## Research Plan pl_003 — Post-1860 Census Survey
**Question q_001:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland; d. 1908, Schuylkill County, PA)?

The prior proof summary (ps_001) rates the conclusion **Probable** and explicitly flags the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses as the primary remaining gap. This plan closes that gap. The 1890 census is correctly excluded — it was largely destroyed in a 1921 fire.

---

### Execution order

| # | Item | Year | Subject | Repository | Note |
|---|------|------|---------|------------|------|
| 1 | pli_007 | 1870 | **Patrick Flynn** | FamilySearch | Primary |
| 2 | pli_008 | 1870 | **Thomas Flynn** | FamilySearch | Primary (FAN) |
| 3 | pli_009 | 1870 | Patrick Flynn | Ancestry | *Fallback for pli_007* |
| 4 | pli_010 | 1880 | **Patrick Flynn** | FamilySearch | **Highest priority** |
| 5 | pli_011 | 1880 | **Thomas Flynn** | FamilySearch | Primary (FAN) |
| 6 | pli_012 | 1880 | Patrick Flynn | Ancestry | *Fallback for pli_010* |
| 7 | pli_013 | 1900 | **Patrick Flynn** | FamilySearch | Primary |
| 8 | pli_014 | 1900 | **Thomas Flynn / widow** | FamilySearch | Primary (FAN) |
| 9 | pli_015 | 1900 | Patrick Flynn | Ancestry | *Fallback for pli_013* |

---

### Rationale by item

**pli_007 — 1870 census, Patrick Flynn, Schuylkill County, FamilySearch**
Patrick is ~25 in 1870 — possibly still with Thomas, possibly on his own. This is the first step to closing the 48-year gap (1860–1908) with no census evidence. Free and indexed.

**pli_008 — 1870 census, Thomas Flynn, Schuylkill County, FamilySearch** *(FAN item)*
Even if Patrick has moved out, Thomas's household maps the sibling group and identifies the spouse (Patrick's putative mother) by name and age. If Patrick IS in Thomas's household, this confirms co-residence and strengthens the inference. Thomas's complete absence from the county prompts a death-record search for the 1860s–1870s.

**pli_009 — 1870 census, Patrick Flynn, Pennsylvania, Ancestry** *(fallback for pli_007)*
Execute only if pli_007 is negative. Ancestry's independent transcription catches variant spellings (Flinn, Flin, Flyn) and broadens scope to all of Pennsylvania in case Patrick relocated.

---

**pli_010 — 1880 census, Patrick Flynn, Schuylkill County, FamilySearch** ⭐ *Highest evidentiary value*
The 1880 census is the **first federal census to record the relationship of every household member to the head** — "son," "boarder," etc. If Patrick (~35) is still in Thomas's household, his stated relationship is *direct evidence* of parentage — capable of moving the conclusion from Probable to **Proved**. Even if Patrick heads his own household, the 1880 schedule records his father's and mother's birthplaces, which would corroborate the Irish-born Thomas Flynn hypothesis. This is the pivot record for the entire question.

**pli_011 — 1880 census, Thomas Flynn, Schuylkill County, FamilySearch** *(FAN item)*
Thomas (~62) may be retired but likely still in the county. His household in 1880 names his wife and all remaining children with explicit relationships — the fullest picture of the Flynn family structure available from any census. His parents' birthplaces also appear, deepening the Irish-origin evidence chain.

**pli_012 — 1880 census, Patrick Flynn, Pennsylvania, Ancestry** *(fallback for pli_010)*
Execute only if pli_010 is negative. Catches spelling variants; broadens to statewide scope.

---

**pli_013 — 1900 census, Patrick Flynn, Schuylkill County, FamilySearch**
Patrick (~55) almost certainly remained in Schuylkill County given his 1908 death there. The 1900 schedule captures month/year of birth, parents' birthplaces, and years in the US. **If Patrick's "father's birthplace" reads Ireland**, that directly corroborates Thomas Flynn (b. Ireland). If Thomas (~82) is living with Patrick, he would be enumerated as "father" — the strongest possible single result.

**pli_014 — 1900 census, Thomas Flynn / widow, Schuylkill County, FamilySearch** *(FAN item)*
Search both Thomas Flynn (if alive) and a widowed Mrs. Flynn of approximately the right age (b. ~1818–1825). If Thomas is absent, the widow's household or another child's household establishes that Thomas died between 1880 and 1900 and opens a targeted death-record search for that window.

**pli_015 — 1900 census, Patrick Flynn, Pennsylvania, Ancestry** *(fallback for pli_013)*
Execute only if pli_013 is negative. Catches spelling variants; broadens to statewide scope.

---

### Fallback relationships

```
pli_007 (FS, 1870, Patrick) ──► pli_009 (Ancestry, 1870, Patrick — if negative)
pli_010 (FS, 1880, Patrick) ──► pli_012 (Ancestry, 1880, Patrick — if negative)
pli_013 (FS, 1900, Patrick) ──► pli_015 (Ancestry, 1900, Patrick — if negative)
```

The Thomas Flynn items (pli_008, pli_011, pli_014) are independent searches run in parallel with their same-year Patrick items, not fallbacks.

---

### Scope summary
**9 searches total:** 6 primary searches (3 Patrick + 3 Thomas/widow) and 3 Ancestry fallbacks. Free repositories (FamilySearch) come before paid (Ancestry). The plan covers all three missing census years and targets both the subject and the putative father independently in each year.

---

**Ready to start?** Say "start searching" and I'll execute pli_007 first — the 1870 FamilySearch search for Patrick Flynn in Schuylkill County.

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn would be approximately 25 in 1870 — old enough to have established his own household but possibly still residing with Thomas Flynn (~52). The 1870 U.S. census is fully indexed on FamilySearch at no cost (NARA microfilm M593). Finding Patrick in 1870 narrows the large gap between the last confirmed placement (1860 Thomas Flynn household, log_004) and his 1908 death, and either confirms or ends co-residence with the putative father. Search Schuylkill County first; if negative, execute fallback pli_009.

Independent search for Thomas Flynn (putative father, ~52 in 1870) in the 1870 Schuylkill County census. Even if Patrick has established his own household by 1870, Thomas's entry still identifies his wife (Patrick's putative mother), any remaining children, and their ages — mapping the sibling group. If Patrick IS enumerated in Thomas's household, this item and pli_007 together confirm co-residence. Thomas's absence from the county would prompt a broader Pennsylvania or adjacent-county search and narrow the window for seeking his death record.

Fallback for pli_007. If Patrick Flynn is not found in Schuylkill County on FamilySearch, Ancestry's independently produced index — using different transcription conventions — may surface him under a spelling variant (Flinn, Flin, Flyn) or with a variant given-name transcription. Also broadens geographic scope to all of Pennsylvania in case Patrick relocated from Schuylkill County between 1860 and 1870.

The 1880 census is the first federal census to record each household member's explicit relationship to the head — 'son,' 'boarder,' 'lodger,' etc. This is the highest-evidentiary-value item in this plan. If Patrick Flynn (~35 in 1880) remains in Thomas Flynn's household, his stated relationship would be direct evidence of parentage, capable of advancing the conclusion from Probable to Proved. Even if Patrick heads his own household, the 1880 schedule records his occupation, marital status, and — critically — his father's and mother's birthplaces, corroborating the Irish-born Thomas Flynn hypothesis. Note: the 1890 census was largely destroyed in a 1921 fire and cannot fill the 1860–1900 gap; the 1880 census is therefore the pivotal record. FamilySearch has the collection indexed at no cost. If negative in Schuylkill County, execute fallback pli_012.

Independent search for Thomas Flynn (~62 in 1880) in Schuylkill County. If Patrick has his own household by 1880, Thomas's entry still names his wife, any remaining children, and their explicit relationships — revealing the full sibling group. The 1880 schedule also records each person's parents' birthplaces, further anchoring the family to Ireland. Thomas's absence from the county in 1880 narrows the death window and directs subsequent research to death, cemetery, or obituary records from the 1860s–1870s.

Fallback for pli_010. If Patrick Flynn is not found in Schuylkill County on FamilySearch in 1880, Ancestry's independent index may surface him under spelling variants (Flinn, Flin) or with a transcription of his given name. Also broadens geographic scope to all of Pennsylvania in case Patrick relocated between 1870 and 1880.

Patrick would be approximately 55 in 1900; his 1908 death in Schuylkill County makes continued local residence highly probable. The 1900 census captures month and year of birth, years of current marriage, parents' birthplaces, and years in the United States. The 'father's birthplace' and 'mother's birthplace' fields are especially valuable: entries of 'Ireland' directly corroborate that Patrick's parents were Irish-born, consistent with Thomas Flynn (b. Ireland per 1850 and 1860 censuses). If Thomas Flynn (~82 if still living in 1900) resides in Patrick's household, he would be enumerated as 'father' — the clearest possible census evidence for the parentage hypothesis. If negative, execute fallback pli_015.

Independent search for Thomas Flynn (~82 in 1900, if still living) and/or his widow in Schuylkill County. If Thomas resides in Patrick's household, the enumerated relationship 'father' is the strongest single census item available for this question. If Thomas is absent, his widow's presence with a child provides a death window — Thomas died between the 1880 census and 1900 — directing subsequent research to death records, obituaries, or cemetery inscriptions in that period. Search both 'Thomas Flynn' and for a widowed Mrs. Flynn of approximately the right age (born ~1818–1825).

Fallback for pli_013. Ancestry's independent 1900 census index uses different transcription conventions and may surface Patrick under spelling variants (Flinn, Flin). Broadens geographic scope to all of Pennsylvania in case Patrick relocated within the state between 1880 and 1900, though his 1908 death in Schuylkill County makes continued local residence the most probable scenario.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The plan correctly applies to the Flynn research scenario. All 9 items address the three missing census years (1870, 1880, 1900) with appropriate record types (all census). Jurisdictions are historically accurate for the period. Rationales correctly explain what each census year reveals (1870 shows Patrick as adult, 1880 records relationships to head and parents' birthplaces, 1900 captures birthplace fields). The skill correctly identified add-new mode and created pl_003 without editing the completed pl_002. All MCP tool calls succeeded with valid schema.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill fully addressed the user request to create a plan for post-1860 censuses (1870, 1880, 1900). All three years are covered with both Patrick Flynn searches and Thomas Flynn searches. Fallback chains are established for all three Patrick searches to Ancestry. The plan includes detailed item rationales, execution order, fallback relationships diagram, and scope summary. No requested elements were omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All 11 MCP tool calls succeeded (matched.kind == 'live'). First call initially failed validation (missing 'items' field) but Claude correctly fixed it on the second attempt by adding an empty items array. All subsequent plan_items appends passed with correct structure: planId 'pl_003', proper sequence numbers (1–9), census record_type, jurisdiction accuracy, date_range values, repository names (FamilySearch/Ancestry), valid fallback_for linkages, and detailed rationales.

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: All 9 items correctly specify census as the record_type. The plan explains why: 1870 narrows the gap between 1860 and 1908 death; 1880 is the first census to record relationship-to-head (directly probative for parentage) and parents' birthplaces; 1900 captures month/year of birth and parental birthplaces. Each rationale ties the census record type to a specific information need (confirming co-residence, corroborating Irish origin, tracking Patrick's household status).

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
dimensions.rationale: Sequence numbers 1–9 reflect proper ordering: free/indexed sources (FamilySearch) before paid (Ancestry) for each year — pli_007 (FS 1870) before pli_009 (Ancestry 1870), pli_010 (FS 1880) before pli_012 (Ancestry 1880), pli_013 (FS 1900) before pli_015 (Ancestry 1900). Fallback_for relationships are explicitly documented in the plan text and fallback diagram. Thomas Flynn items (pli_008, pli_011, pli_014) are marked as FAN items and run in parallel with same-year Patrick searches, not as fallbacks. Rationales explain placement (e.g., pli_010 marked as 'Highest priority' due to relationship-to-head field).

dimensions.name: Jurisdiction accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Jurisdictions are historically accurate. Primary searches target 'Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania' (where Patrick was living in 1860 and died in 1908; county existed throughout 1870–1900). Fallback searches broaden to 'Pennsylvania' state-level to catch relocated records. The plan correctly notes the 1890 census was 'largely destroyed in a 1921 fire' and excludes it. No anachronistic jurisdictions (pre-statehood territories, defunct counties) appear.

dimensions.name: Plan mode and lifecycle
dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly selected add-new mode: pl_002 is completed, q_001 is still in_progress (not proved), and no active plan exists for the next research phase. The new plan pl_003 is set to status 'active'. The prior plan pl_002 was not edited in place (its status remains 'completed'). Exactly one active plan now exists for q_001. The audit trail of completed plans is preserved.

---

## Research Plan pl_002 — *Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?*

**⚠ Starting-point note (BCG Standard 11):** The only evidence linking Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father is co-enumeration in the 1850 census (indirect evidence, hypothesis h_001). Patrick's mother is entirely unknown. Three sources already cited in `tree.gedcomx.json` — the 1860 census (S2), death certificate (S3), and probate records (S4) — have **no corresponding log entries in research.json** and must be formally searched before they can count as evidence.

---

### Execution order

| # | Item | Record Type | Repository | Rationale |
|---|------|-------------|------------|-----------|
| **1** | **pli_004** | Death certificate (1908) | FamilySearch | Patrick died 12 Mar 1908, after Pennsylvania's 1906 statewide death registration began. His certificate names both parents directly — **the single highest-value source for this question**. Free, indexed ("PA, Death Certificates, 1906–1968"). |
| **2** | pli_005 *(fallback for pli_004)* | Death certificate (1908) | Ancestry | Independent index of the same originals; catches spelling variants (Flyn, Flinn) and cross-checks any record found on FamilySearch. |
| **3** | **pli_006** | 1860 census | FamilySearch | Patrick (~15) should still be in Thomas's household. A second co-enumeration transforms a single data point into a consistent pattern. Must be formally documented — tree cites this source (S2, dwelling 112) but research.json has no log entry. |
| **4** | **pli_007** | 1880 census | FamilySearch | Introduces the relationship-to-head column. Patrick (~35) and Thomas (~62) should both appear; column may state "son/father" explicitly. Thomas's entry may also name a wife — the first clue to Patrick's mother. |
| **5** | **pli_008** | Probate — Thomas Flynn's estate (1860–1910) | FamilySearch | A will naming Patrick as heir is **direct evidence of parentage**. FamilySearch indexed collection "PA, Probate Records, 1683–1994." Also search Patrick's own estate, which may reference parents. Tree cites S4 but no log entry exists. |
| **6** | pli_009 *(fallback for pli_008)* | Probate — browse-only | FamilySearch | Image group 007936749: Schuylkill probate 1851–1930, 412 images, 0% indexed. Browse directly if Thomas Flynn is not in the index. |
| **7** | **pli_010** | Church records | FamilySearch | Irish Catholic family. FS collection 1401638 + browse-only image group 007936751 (Schuylkill church records 1842–1895, 187 images, 0% indexed). A Patrick baptism entry names both parents; a Thomas Flynn marriage entry names his wife. |
| **8** | pli_011 *(fallback for pli_010)* | Church records | Diocese of Scranton / other | Diocese of Scranton Archives (est. 1868) holds undigitized Schuylkill Catholic registers. Diocese of Philadelphia for pre-1868 records. Contact directly if FamilySearch yields nothing. |
| **9** | **pli_012** | Naturalization records (court) | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn's declaration of intention and petition (Schuylkill courts). Browse-only image group 007936750 (1849–1915, 298 images, 0% indexed). Also Ancestry "PA Naturalization Records, 1794–1931." May name Thomas's wife and arrival details — **key FAN-cluster item for identifying Patrick's mother**. |
| **10** | **pli_013** | Marriage records | FamilySearch | Patrick's marriage certificate names his father. FS collection 1921317 (indexed, 1885–1950) + browse-only volume 007936753 (22% indexed, 1870–1910). Also search Thomas Flynn's marriage — directly names Patrick's mother. |

---

### Fallback relationships
- If **pli_004** (death cert, FamilySearch) finds nothing → search **pli_005** (Ancestry)
- If **pli_008** (probate, indexed) finds nothing → browse **pli_009** (unindexed volume)
- If **pli_010** (church records, FamilySearch) finds nothing → contact **pli_011** (Diocese of Scranton)

### Scope
10 searches across 4 record types (vital records, census, probate, church), plus 2 supplementary (court/naturalization, marriage). Estimated 6–8 active searches given the fallback structure.

---

**Ready to start?** The first step is pli_004 — Patrick Flynn's 1908 death certificate on FamilySearch ("Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968"). Would you like me to execute the plan now?

items.rationale: Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County (tree F2). Pennsylvania began statewide death registration in 1906, so his certificate falls in the FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968.' Death certificates from this period typically name both parents — father's name and mother's maiden name — making this the single most direct source available for q_001. Free access, fully indexed. Note: tree.gedcomx.json cites S3 (death cert. no. 4521) as a source for parent-child relationship R1, but no log entry in research.json documents this search; it must be formally executed.

Ancestry's 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1963' is an independent index of the same originals. Search here if pli_004 (FamilySearch) yields no result — Ancestry may capture surname variants (Flyn, Flinn) missed by FamilySearch transcription — or to cross-check any record found.

Patrick would be approximately 15 in 1860 and almost certainly still living in Thomas Flynn's household. Finding him there a second time elevates the co-enumeration evidence from one data point to a consistent pattern across two census years, strengthening hypothesis h_001. Note: tree.gedcomx.json cites the 1860 census (S2, dwelling 112) as a source for parent-child relationship R1, but no log entry or assertion in research.json documents this search — it must be formally executed and recorded.

The 1880 census introduced a relationship-to-head column enabling direct family reconstruction. Patrick (~35) and Thomas (~62) should both appear. If they share a household the column may explicitly record son/father. Even in separate households, Thomas's entry may name a wife — the first clue to Patrick's mother's identity — and consistent ages and Irish birthplaces across three census years would further corroborate h_001.

Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) would have died sometime between roughly 1860 and 1910. A will naming Patrick as a son is direct evidence of parentage; letters of administration listing heirs would be strong indirect evidence. FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' covers Schuylkill County. Also search for Patrick Flynn's own estate — a filing may reference his parents. Note: tree.gedcomx.json cites Schuylkill County Probate Records (S4) as a source for R1, but no log entry documents this search.

Browse-only image group 007936749 contains Schuylkill County probate records 1851-1930 (412 images, 0% indexed). If the FamilySearch indexed search (pli_008) returns no result for Thomas Flynn — because the record is unindexed or the name is poorly transcribed — browse this volume directly. It spans the full window in which Thomas (born ~1818) would have died.

The Flynn family was almost certainly Irish Catholic. FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638) includes Schuylkill County Catholic parish registers; browse-only image group 007936751 covers Schuylkill church records 1842-1895 (187 images, 0% indexed). A baptismal entry for Patrick would name both parents directly. A marriage entry for Thomas Flynn would name his wife (Patrick's mother). Pre-civil-registration Catholic registers are the primary substitute for missing vital records before 1906.

The Diocese of Scranton (established 1868) holds original sacramental registers for Schuylkill County Catholic parishes not yet digitized (dioceseofscranton.org). If FamilySearch (pli_010) yields no baptism or marriage record, contact the Diocese of Scranton Archives directly. For pre-1868 records the Diocese of Philadelphia Archives holds the earlier registers.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland (birthplace Ireland, 1850 census a_002). He would have filed a declaration of intention and petition for naturalization in a Schuylkill County court. FamilySearch browse-only image group 007936750 covers Schuylkill naturalization records 1849-1915 (298 images, 0% indexed); also search Ancestry 'Pennsylvania, U.S., Naturalization Records, 1794-1931.' His papers may name his wife, origin, and arrival date — potentially the first direct evidence for Patrick's mother. FAN-cluster item targeting Thomas Flynn (I2).

If Patrick Flynn married in Schuylkill County, his marriage certificate or license would name his father. FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885-1950' (collection 1921317) is indexed; browse-only volume 007936753 covers Schuylkill marriages 1870-1910 (22% indexed). Also search for Thomas Flynn's marriage — if found, it directly names Patrick's mother. Patrick was adult by ~1866 and died in 1908, so his marriage most likely falls 1866-1895.

Pre-civil-registration Catholic registers are the primary substitute for missing vital records before 1906.

The Diocese of Scranton (established 1868) holds original sacramental registers for Schuylkill County Catholic parishes not yet digitized (dioceseofscranton.org). If FamilySearch (pli_010) yields no baptism or marriage record, contact the Diocese of Scranton Archives directly. For pre-1868 records the Diocese of Philadelphia Archives holds the earlier registers.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland (birthplace Ireland, 1850 census a_002). He would have filed a declaration of intention and petition for naturalization in a Schuylkill County court. FamilySearch browse-only image group 007936750 covers Schuylkill naturalization records 1849-1915 (298 images, 0% indexed); also search Ancestry 'Pennsylvania, U.S., Naturalization Records, 1794-1931.' His papers may name his wife, origin, and arrival date — potentially the first direct evidence for Patrick's mother. FAN-cluster item targeting Thomas Flynn (I2).

If Patrick Flynn married in Schuylkill County, his marriage certificate or license would name his father. FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885-1950' (collection 1921317) is indexed; browse-only volume 007936753 covers Schuylkill marriages 1870-1910 (22% indexed). Also search for Thomas Flynn's marriage — if found, it directly names Patrick's mother. Patrick was adult by ~1866 and died in 1908, so his marriage most likely falls 1866-1895.

items.rationale: Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County (tree F2). Pennsylvania began statewide death registration in 1906, so his certificate falls in the FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968.' Death certificates from this period typically name both parents — father's name and mother's maiden name — making this the single most direct source available for q_001. Free access, fully indexed. Note: tree.gedcomx.json cites S3 (death cert. no. 4521) as a source for parent-child relationship R1, but no log entry in research.json documents this search; it must be formally executed.

Ancestry's 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1963' is an independent index of the same originals. Search here if pli_004 (FamilySearch) yields no result — Ancestry may capture surname variants (Flyn, Flinn) missed by FamilySearch transcription — or to cross-check any record found.

Patrick would be approximately 15 in 1860 and almost certainly still living in Thomas Flynn's household. Finding him there a second time elevates the co-enumeration evidence from one data point to a consistent pattern across two census years, strengthening hypothesis h_001. Note: tree.gedcomx.json cites the 1860 census (S2, dwelling 112) as a source for parent-child relationship R1, but no log entry or assertion in research.json documents this search — it must be formally executed and recorded.

The 1880 census introduced a relationship-to-head column enabling direct family reconstruction. Patrick (~35) and Thomas (~62) should both appear. If they share a household the column may explicitly record son/father. Even in separate households, Thomas's entry may name a wife — the first clue to Patrick's mother's identity — and consistent ages and Irish birthplaces across three census years would further corroborate h_001.

Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) would have died sometime between roughly 1860 and 1910. A will naming Patrick as a son is direct evidence of parentage; letters of administration listing heirs would be strong indirect evidence. FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' covers Schuylkill County. Also search for Patrick Flynn's own estate — a filing may reference his parents. Note: tree.gedcomx.json cites Schuylkill County Probate Records (S4) as a source for R1, but no log entry documents this search.

If the FamilySearch indexed search (pli_008) returns no result for Thomas Flynn — because the record is unindexed or the name is poorly transcribed — browse this volume directly. It spans the full window in which Thomas (born ~1818) would have died.

Key findings:
- **FamilySearch** has "Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985" (Catholic parishes) and "Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994" (browse-only — explains the log_006 negative on FS)
- **External links** (Ancestry) are thin for Schuylkill — confirms I need to plan broadly
- **Wiki** confirms Irish Catholic miners dominated Schuylkill County; coal company payroll records survive at the Historical Society of Schuylkill County
- **Both pl_001 and pl_002 are `completed`**, proof tier is only `probable` → **add-new mode**

Planned ID assignments: pl_003 (new plan shell), pli_007–pli_015 (nine items). Writing in one batched call now.The batched format hit a schema validation. I'll write the plan shell first, then add items sequentially.Plan shell `pl_003` created. Now I'll add all nine items in parallel — none reference each other's IDs, so parallel calls are safe.All 9 items written and validated. Here's the complete plan:

---

## Research Plan pl_003 — *Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?*

**Context:** Two prior plans (pl_001, pl_002) are completed. The proof summary sits at **Probable** — Thomas Flynn is the likely father, supported by two census co-residence records and a death certificate, but no direct evidence has yet been found. The 1870/1880/1900 censuses, church records, and probate on Ancestry are the clearest remaining paths.

---

### Execution order

| # | ID | Record type | Repository | Jurisdiction / Period | Why |
|---|-----|-------------|------------|-----------------------|-----|
| 1 | pli_007 | Census | FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co., PA — 1870 | Earliest unexecuted post-war census; establishes whether Thomas was alive in 1870 and whether Patrick was still in his household |
| 2 | pli_008 | Census | FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co., PA — 1880 | **Highest-value search:** 1880 is the first census with a relationship-to-head column — a "son" or "father" entry is near-direct evidence of parentage |
| 3 | pli_009 | Census | FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co., PA — 1900 | Patrick (~55) in his own household; 1900 records parents' birthplaces and fills the 1860–1908 timeline gap |
| 4 | pli_010 | Church | FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co., PA — 1845–1880 | Irish Catholic community; FS collection 1401638 ("Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records") holds baptism, marriage, and burial registers — a baptism naming Thomas as father is the strongest possible evidence type |
| 5 | pli_011 | Immigration | FamilySearch | USA ports — 1845–1850 | Great Famine emigration; a passenger manifest with the Flynn family group names the mother and directly corroborates Thomas–Patrick relationship |
| 6 | pli_012 | Probate | Ancestry | Schuylkill Co., PA — 1870–1910 | **Fallback from pli_006** — FamilySearch's PA probate collection is browse-only (0 indexed persons); Ancestry has independent indexes covering intestate administrations and estate inventories |
| 7 | pli_013 | Military | FamilySearch | Pennsylvania — 1861–1880 | Patrick was Civil War service age (16–20); pension files name parents and birthplace |
| 8 | pli_014 | Newspaper | Ancestry | Schuylkill Co., PA — 1908 | Miners' Journal obituary for Patrick's March 1908 death may name parents, siblings, and Irish birthplace |
| 9 | pli_015 | Land | FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co., PA — 1850–1890 | FAN cluster — deed books for Thomas Flynn as grantor/grantee; transfers to children are direct evidence; deed witnesses identify neighbors and relatives |

---

### Fallback relationship
If step 6 (Ancestry probate) also returns nothing, the plan will need a supplemental item for a physical visit to the Schuylkill County Register of Wills to browse unindexed estate files.

### Scope
**9 searches** across 6 record types and 3 repositories (FamilySearch, Ancestry, FamilySearch Catalog/county).

---

**Ready to start?** The natural first step is the 1880 census (pli_008) — it carries the highest probability of advancing the proof tier from *Probable* to *Proved* in a single search. Say "start searching" and I'll hand off to the search-records skill.

args.query: Pennsylvania Catholic church records Irish immigrant genealogy Schuylkill County

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn (~25) and Thomas Flynn (~52) should both appear in the 1870 census — the earliest unexecuted post-Civil War census. Though the 1870 census lacks a relationship column, finding both men in the same household corroborates the parentage hypothesis; Thomas's absence would indicate he died before 1870 and narrow the probate search window.

The 1880 census is the first U.S. census to record each person's relationship to the head of household. If Patrick appears in Thomas Flynn's household listed as 'son,' or Thomas appears in Patrick's household as 'father,' that is near-direct evidence of the parent-child relationship — the highest-value unexecuted search remaining in this project. Fully indexed on FamilySearch.

Patrick Flynn (~55) in his own 1900 household. The 1900 census records parents' birthplaces for each person, which would add detail about the Irish origin of Thomas and Patrick's mother. If Thomas survived to 1900, he may appear in Patrick's household. Patrick's wife's duration of marriage and number of children living help fill the 1860-1908 timeline gap.

Irish coal-mining immigrants in Schuylkill County were overwhelmingly Catholic. FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985') includes baptism, marriage, and burial registers from Schuylkill County Catholic parishes. A baptism record for Patrick naming Thomas Flynn as father is direct, primary evidence of parentage — the strongest evidence type available. Patrick's marriage record would name both parents; Thomas's burial would fix his death date.

Patrick was born ~1845 in Ireland and was in Schuylkill County by the 1850 census (age 5). The Flynn family almost certainly emigrated during the Great Famine (~1845-1849), likely entering via Philadelphia or New York. Passenger manifests from this period sometimes list family groups traveling together — which would identify Thomas Flynn with a wife and children including Patrick, naming the mother and directly corroborating the Thomas-Patrick relationship.

The FamilySearch probate search (log_006 / pli_006) was negative; however, FamilySearch's Pennsylvania probate collection (1417683) has 2.3 million images but zero indexed persons — it is browse-only, making keyword searches unreliable. Ancestry holds independently indexed Pennsylvania probate records including administrative letters, intestate proceedings, and estate inventories. The date range extends to 1910 to account for Thomas potentially surviving past 1900. A will or administration naming Patrick as a son or heir is direct evidence of parentage.

Patrick Flynn (born ~1845) was of Civil War military age (16-20 in 1861-1865). Pension files — especially applications by Patrick or a surviving widow — routinely include affidavits naming parents, birthplace, and early life. Thomas Flynn (~43-48 in 1861-1865) may also have served. FamilySearch indexes Civil War pension and service records for Pennsylvania, including the General Index to Pension Files 1861-1934.

Patrick died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County. The Miners' Journal (Pottsville) and other local papers regularly published obituaries naming surviving family, siblings, and sometimes parents or place of birth. Ancestry's historical newspaper collections (via Newspapers.com) cover Pennsylvania for this period. An obituary could name Patrick's parents, surviving siblings, and Irish birthplace — corroborating evidence and identifying FAN members for further searching.

FAN cluster search — Schuylkill County deed books for Thomas Flynn as grantor or grantee, 1850-1890. Land transfers to children provide direct evidence of parent-child relationships; co-grantors often identify siblings. Witnesses on deeds are frequently neighbors or relatives whose own records may contain evidence about the Flynn family. FamilySearch holds Pennsylvania land records; Schuylkill County courthouse holds the originals.

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn (~25) and Thomas Flynn (~52) should both appear in the 1870 census — the earliest unexecuted post-Civil War census. Though the 1870 census lacks a relationship column, finding both men in the same household corroborates the parentage hypothesis; Thomas's absence would indicate he died before 1870 and narrow the probate search window.

The 1880 census is the first U.S. census to record each person's relationship to the head of household. If Patrick appears in Thomas Flynn's household listed as 'son,' or Thomas appears in Patrick's household as 'father,' that is near-direct evidence of the parent-child relationship — the highest-value unexecuted search remaining in this project. Fully indexed on FamilySearch.

Patrick Flynn (~55) in his own 1900 household. The 1900 census records parents' birthplaces for each person, which would add detail about the Irish origin of Thomas and Patrick's mother. If Thomas survived to 1900, he may appear in Patrick's household. Patrick's wife's duration of marriage and number of children living help fill the 1860-1908 timeline gap.

Irish coal-mining immigrants in Schuylkill County were overwhelmingly Catholic. FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985') includes baptism, marriage, and burial registers from Schuylkill County Catholic parishes. A baptism record for Patrick naming Thomas Flynn as father is direct, primary evidence of parentage — the strongest evidence type available. Patrick's marriage record would name both parents; Thomas's burial would fix his death date.

Patrick was born ~1845 in Ireland and was in Schuylkill County by the 1850 census (age 5). The Flynn family almost certainly emigrated during the Great Famine (~1845-1849), likely entering via Philadelphia or New York. Passenger manifests from this period sometimes list family groups traveling together — which would identify Thomas Flynn with a wife and children including Patrick, naming the mother and directly corroborating the Thomas-Patrick relationship.

The FamilySearch probate search (log_006 / pli_006) was negative; however, FamilySearch's Pennsylvania probate collection (1417683) has 2.3 million images but zero indexed persons — it is browse-only, making keyword searches unreliable. Ancestry holds independently indexed Pennsylvania probate records including administrative letters, intestate proceedings, and estate inventories. The date range extends to 1910 to account for Thomas potentially surviving past 1900. A will or administration naming Patrick as a son or heir is direct evidence of parentage.

Patrick Flynn (born ~1845) was of Civil War military age (16-20 in 1861-1865). Pension files — especially applications by Patrick or a surviving widow — routinely include affidavits naming parents, birthplace, and early life. Thomas Flynn (~43-48 in 1861-1865) may also have served. FamilySearch indexes Civil War pension and service records for Pennsylvania, including the General Index to Pension Files 1861-1934.

Patrick died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County. The Miners' Journal (Pottsville) and other local papers regularly published obituaries naming surviving family, siblings, and sometimes parents or place of birth. Ancestry's historical newspaper collections (via Newspapers.com) cover Pennsylvania for this period. An obituary could name Patrick's parents, surviving siblings, and Irish birthplace — corroborating evidence and identifying FAN members for further searching.

FAN cluster search — Schuylkill County deed books for Thomas Flynn as grantor or grantee, 1850-1890. Land transfers to children provide direct evidence of parent-child relationships; co-grantors often identify siblings. Witnesses on deeds are frequently neighbors or relatives whose own records may contain evidence about the Flynn family. FamilySearch holds Pennsylvania land records; Schuylkill County courthouse holds the originals.

## Revised Research Plan — `pl_003` (supersedes `pl_002`)

**Question:** `q_001` — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?
**Reason for re-plan:** New reference suggests Thomas Flynn died ~1865, not the 1875–1890 window targeted in `pl_002`. All date ranges have been shifted to the 1860–1870 period accordingly.

---

### Plan Items

| # | ID | Record Type | Date Range | Repository | Status |
|---|-----|-------------|------------|------------|--------|
| 1 | `pli_007` | Probate | **1860–1870** | FamilySearch | planned |
| 2 | `pli_008` | Census (1870) | **1870** | FamilySearch | planned |
| 3 | `pli_009` | Church (burial) | **1860–1870** | FamilySearch | planned |
| 4 | `pli_010` | Newspaper | **1862–1868** | Other (Newspapers.com / Chronicling America) | planned |
| 5 | `pli_011` | Tax | **1860–1870** | FamilySearch | planned *(fallback for pli_007)* |

---

### Rationale & Execution Order

1. **`pli_007` — Probate, FamilySearch (1860–1870).** First and highest-yield item. Schuylkill County wills and administrations are indexed on FamilySearch. A will or administration filed after a ~1865 death would fall squarely in this window. Directly supersedes the in-progress `pli_006` (which was targeting the wrong decade).

2. **`pli_008` — 1870 Census, FamilySearch.** If Thomas died ~1865 he should be *absent* from the 1870 census. His absence corroborates the earlier death estimate; his widow appearing with children is a valuable FAN lead. If he appears *alive*, the death date assumption needs revisiting before continuing.

3. **`pli_009` — Church burial records, FamilySearch (1860–1870).** The Flynn family was almost certainly Irish Catholic; St. Mary's Pottsville was established by the 1840s. Burial registers from the 1860s are partially digitized on FamilySearch and could document Thomas's death directly without a will.

4. **`pli_010` — Miners' Journal death notices (1862–1868), Newspapers.com / Chronicling America.** The Miners' Journal (Pottsville) regularly published death notices. A notice in the ~1862–1868 window could fix the date and may name surviving children including Patrick.

5. **`pli_011` — Tax records, FamilySearch (1860–1870) — fallback for `pli_007`.** If probate yields nothing, tax assessment lists will show the last year Thomas was taxed as a living resident. His disappearance from the rolls, followed by a widow or estate entry, would bracket the death year to within 1–2 years.

---
